sound_type: the submerge call -- a loud, cuckoo-like 'cow-cow-cow-cow-cow-cow-cow-cowp-cowp-cowp' that starts slow and accelerates into a chattering climax
ecosystem: lake edge, pond, marsh, slow river -- freshwater with emergent vegetation
season: year-round resident in western Washington (some seasonal movement)

csv_substitution.reason: SPS Part B engine is birds-and-mammals only. Pied-billed Grebe replaces Green Frog, preserving the original's lake edge habitat and E=4 energy level. The Green Frog produces a distinctive 'banjo plunk' call from the vegetated margins of freshwater lakes (E=4, Ranidae). The Pied-billed Grebe produces a loud cuckoo-like 'cow-cow-cow' series from the same lake-edge habitat (E=4, Podicipedidae). Both are vocally distinctive, both occupy lake-edge vegetation, both are quiet relative to their surroundings but unmistakable when they call. Sixteenth consecutive CSV substitution replacing an amphibian-class species (Pacific Chorus Frog at 45, Columbia Spotted Frog at 46, Red-legged Frog at 47, Tailed Frog at 48, Western Toad at 49, Rough-skinned Newt at 50, Northwestern Salamander at 51, Long-toed Salamander at 52, Olympic Torrent Salamander at 53, Clouded Salamander at 54, Oregon Slender Salamander at 55, Ensatina at 56, Pacific Giant Salamander at 57, Red-legged Frog at 58, Bullfrog at 59, Green Frog at 60).
preserved: vocally distinctive presence at freshwater margins

coordinates.description: Union Bay Natural Area / Montlake Fill reference; common on sheltered freshwater lakes and marshes with emergent vegetation throughout western Washington lowlands. Found at Union Bay, Nisqually NWR, Ridgefield NWR, Juanita Bay, and similar freshwater marshes with cattails and bulrushes.

taxonomy_note.description: Pied-billed Grebe introduces BOTH a new family (Podicipedidae) AND a new order (Podicipediformes) to the engine at degree 60. First grebe. Fourth new order in five degrees (56 Columbiformes, 57 Coraciiformes, 58 Anseriformes, 60 Podicipediformes). Genus Podilymbus is NEW -- a contraction of Podiceps + colymbus (diver). Podiceps from Latin podex (rump) + pes (foot). The full binomial translates to approximately 'rump-footed rump-foot' -- one of the most tautological names in ornithology. Passeriformes percentage drops to 80.3% (49 of 61 entries). Fifth consecutive non-passerine (56-60) -- the longest non-passerine streak in the engine.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Podilymbus = contraction of Podiceps (grebe type genus, from Latin podex 'rump' + pes 'foot') + Latin colymbus ('diver'). podiceps = Latin podex ('rump') + pes ('foot'). Full translation: 'rump-footed rump-foot' or 'the diving rump-foot whose feet are at its rump.' Common name 'Pied-billed' refers to the breeding-season bill: pale with a prominent black vertical band. 'Grebe' from French grebe, possibly from Breton krib ('crest').
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Podilymbus is new to the engine at degree 60. A small New World genus containing only two species: P. podiceps (Pied-billed Grebe, widespread) and P. gigas (Atitlan Grebe, EXTINCT since 1986 -- hunted, habitat loss, competition from introduced bass in Lake Atitlan, Guatemala). The genus Podilymbus thus contains one living species and one extinction within living memory.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Podicipediformes appears for the 1st time at degree 60. Fourth new order in five degrees (56-60). Grebes are an ancient lineage, sister to Phoenicopteriformes (flamingos) in the clade Mirandornithes. The closest relatives of the small, brown, freshwater diver are not ducks but flamingos. After degree 60: 49 of 61 entries (80.3%) are Passeriformes -- dropping below 81% for the first time. Five consecutive non-passerine species (56-60).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Podicipedidae enters the engine at degree 60 as the 26th family. First grebe. Approximately 22 species worldwide, characterized by lobed feet, legs set far back, dense waterproof plumage, vestigial tail, feather-eating behavior, and floating nests.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Primary 'cow-cow-cow' call: 500-2,000 Hz, hollow resonant series that accelerates. Also: soft 'kuk-kuk-kuk' contact call (1,000-2,500 Hz), sharp 'whinny' or 'kee-ow' alarm (1,500-3,000 Hz). Both sexes vocalize.
song_metrics.note_structure: ACCELERATING SERIES: The primary call begins with slow, distinct 'cow' notes at ~2-3 per second, then accelerates progressively to ~5-6 per second, with notes shortening and sharpening as tempo increases. The series climaxes in a rapid chattering and stops abruptly. The acceleration is diagnostic for Pied-billed Grebe.
song_metrics.note_types: Moderate vocalization diversity. Primary 'cow-cow' territorial/advertising series (both sexes), contact 'kuk' notes (both sexes), alarm 'whinny/kee-ow' (both sexes), chick begging calls (nestlings). Duetting between pairs is common -- both members call simultaneously or in alternation during territory establishment. Vocalizations are innate.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: The accelerating cow-cow series IS the phrase: a single ramp from slow to fast, building rhythmic tension that resolves through velocity rather than pitch. No melodic development, no tonal variation within the series. The phrase says everything through tempo: beginning slow and measured, ending in near-frantic repetition. Structurally the opposite of the Mallard's descrescendo (which begins loud and fades) -- the Grebe begins deliberate and accelerates.
song_metrics.timbre: Hollow. Resonant. Cuckoo-like. The individual notes have a quality that surprises listeners -- it sounds tropical, primate-like, or like a distant cuckoo. Not thin or breathy like the Wood Duck whistle, not harsh or nasal like the Mallard quack. The timbre is round, open-voweled, and carries well over flat water. First-time listeners often cannot identify the source or even guess that the sound comes from a bird.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- surprisingly loud for such a small, secretive bird. Audible at 200-400 meters across open water. The call carries well in the calm conditions of sheltered lake edges and marsh openings. Louder than the Wood Duck (E=3) but quieter than the Mallard (E=5). The effective acoustic footprint is reduced by the bird's secretive behavior -- it may call only a few times then go silent for long periods.
song_metrics.tempo: ACCELERATING -- the defining temporal property. The series begins at ~2-3 notes per second and accelerates to ~5-6 notes per second. This is the first accelerating acoustic element in the engine -- a new tempo-direction category. Previous elements have had pitch direction (ascending, descending) and amplitude direction (crescendo, descrescendo). The Grebe adds tempo direction to the engine's acoustic vocabulary.
song_metrics.context: Primary cow-cow series given for territory establishment, mate attraction, and pair-bond maintenance. Both sexes call. Duetting is common during territory establishment. Contact 'kuk' notes given between pair members and between parent and chicks. Alarm calls given when predators approach nest. Vocalizations most intense during breeding season (March-July) but occur year-round on territory.
song_metrics.regional_variation: MINIMAL geographic variation. Vocalizations are innate. No dialects, no cultural learning. Like the Wood Duck, Mallard, Kingfisher, and Band-tailed Pigeon before it, the Grebe's call is genetically determined. Five consecutive non-passerine species (56-60), all with innate vocalizations.
song_metrics.key_property: THE SUBMERGE CALL -- the 61st acoustic element (species side). A loud, cuckoo-like, accelerating series 'cow-cow-cow-cow-cowp-cowp-cowp' that builds in tempo from deliberate to frantic. Named 'the submerge call' for the paradox: the bird produces a loud, carrying, attention-grabbing vocalization and then sinks silently beneath the surface. Sound followed by disappearance. Three resonances: (1) acceleration -- the first tempo-direction element in the engine; (2) mismatch -- the exotic sound from the small drab bird; (3) disappearance -- the 61st element is the first whose defining context is its own erasure.

plumage.overall: Small, compact, chunky waterbird -- smaller than Wood Duck or Mallard. Drab brown throughout, no bright colors, no iridescence, no bold patterns. The 'pied bill' (pale with black band) is the diagnostic mark. Minimal sexual dimorphism -- males and females nearly identical. After two consecutive degrees of extreme dimorphism (Wood Duck baroque, Mallard graphic), the Grebe arrives with no display plumage at all.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: BREEDING: Thick, short, pale bill with prominent BLACK VERTICAL BAND across the middle. Black chin and throat patch. NON-BREEDING: Bill band fades, chin patch reduced. Both plumages: compact shape, virtually no visible tail, rides low in water. The 'pied bill' is the namesake field mark.
plumage.upperparts: Dark brown, somewhat uniform. No patterning, no spots, no streaks. Functional camouflage against dark water and reeds.
plumage.underparts: Paler brown to grayish-brown. Slightly lighter than upperparts but not contrastingly so.
plumage.head: Dark brown crown. Paler brown sides. Black chin and throat patch in breeding plumage. No crest, no eye ring, no supercilium. The face is plain. The bill is the only striking feature.
plumage.bill: Short, thick, laterally compressed -- unlike any duck bill. Pale (whitish to bluish-gray) with prominent black vertical band during breeding season. Band fades in non-breeding plumage. The bill is a crushing tool for hard-shelled prey (crayfish, snails), not a filtering tool like the Mallard's lamellate bill.
plumage.tail: Vestigial. Essentially absent. The body terminates in a rounded, fluffy rear end with no visible tail feathers in the field. This is the most obvious structural difference from ducks: ducks have prominent tails; grebes do not.
plumage.feet: Lobed, NOT webbed. Each toe has individual lateral flaps that expand during power stroke and fold during recovery stroke. Legs set extremely far back on body -- optimized for diving, useless for walking. Greenish-gray. The fundamental anatomical distinction between Podicipedidae and Anatidae.
plumage.field_id: Small, compact, brown waterbird (31-38 cm, 253-568 g) that rides LOW in the water with virtually no visible tail. Chunky proportions. Thick pale bill with BLACK VERTICAL BAND (breeding). Brown overall with black chin/throat (breeding). Often seen floating among reeds at lake edges or disappearing by sinking rather than flying. Dives frequently. Not a duck despite superficial resemblance. The pied bill is diagnostic.

nodes.title: The Submerge Call -- 61st Acoustic Element
nodes.description: A loud, cuckoo-like accelerating series 'cow-cow-cow-cow-cowp-cowp-cowp' that builds in tempo from deliberate to frantic. E=4. Named for the paradox: the bird produces a loud, carrying call and then sinks silently beneath the surface. Sound followed by disappearance. The first tempo-direction element in the engine -- not pitch rising or falling but tempo accelerating. Music-theory parallel: an accelerando passage that builds tension through velocity rather than volume or pitch.

nodes.title: New Order: Podicipediformes -- Fourth in Five Degrees
nodes.description: The 9th or 10th order in the engine. Grebes enter for the first time. Four new orders in five degrees (56 Columbiformes, 57 Coraciiformes, 58 Anseriformes, 60 Podicipediformes). The engine's order diversity is expanding at its fastest rate. Five consecutive non-passerine species (56-60) -- the longest non-passerine streak. Passeriformes drops to 80.3%. Grebes are sister to flamingos (Mirandornithes) -- the closest relatives of the small brown diver are tall pink waders.

nodes.title: New Family: Podicipedidae -- 26th Family, First Grebe
nodes.description: The grebe family enters the engine. Approximately 22 species worldwide. Characterized by lobed feet, rear-mounted legs, dense waterproof plumage, vestigial tail, feather-eating behavior, and floating nests. The 26th family across 61 entries. The genus Podilymbus contains only two species: P. podiceps (widespread) and P. gigas (Atitlan Grebe -- EXTINCT since 1986). The engine's first grebe genus contains a living species and a recent extinction.

nodes.title: Sinking Without Diving -- Controlled Buoyancy
nodes.description: The Pied-billed Grebe can gradually sink beneath the surface without diving -- compressing plumage to expel trapped air, reducing buoyancy, and descending silently like a submarine. No splash, no movement, no ripple. Only the head may remain above water, periscope-like. No duck can do this. The grebe's primary alarm response: where Wood Duck flies and Mallard flies or swims, the Grebe sinks. Becoming less visible rather than more active. The opposite of every other waterbird in the engine.

nodes.title: NOT a Duck -- Convergent Evolution
nodes.description: Despite swimming on the same ponds as ducks, the Pied-billed Grebe is NOT related to ducks. Podicipediformes and Anseriformes are entirely separate orders. The resemblance is convergent evolution: independent lineages evolving similar body plans for similar environments. Fundamental differences: lobed feet vs. webbed, vestigial tail vs. functional tail, adjustable buoyancy vs. fixed, cannot walk vs. walks freely. The engine places two degrees of true ducks (58-59) followed by one degree of not-duck (60): same pond, different evolutionary history. Appearance is not identity.

nodes.title: Three Consecutive Aquatic-Primary Species (58-59-60)
nodes.description: Wood Duck (surface specialist, E=3), Mallard (surface generalist, E=5), Pied-billed Grebe (sustained diver, E=4). Three degrees, two orders, three aquatic strata: surface, subsurface tip, full submersion. The engine descends through the water column. Nesting matches: tree cavity (58), ground (59), floating platform (60) -- from arboreal to terrestrial to aquatic. Naming: beauty (Aix sponsa), function (Anas platyrhynchos), anatomy (Podilymbus podiceps).

nodes.title: First Sustained Diver -- Lives Underwater
nodes.description: The engine's first bird that stays underwater as a primary feeding strategy. Dives 20-30 seconds, propelled by lobed feet, pursuing fish and crayfish. Three underwater strategies now documented: walking (American Dipper, 52), striking (Belted Kingfisher, 57), swimming (Pied-billed Grebe, 60). The Dipper walks stream bottoms. The Kingfisher plunge-dives for 1-2 seconds. The Grebe dives and hunts for half a minute. Three different underwater worlds in eight degrees.

nodes.title: Feather Eating -- Internal Feltworks
nodes.description: Grebes eat their own feathers. Habitually. The feathers form a feltlike mass in the stomach that protects the intestines from sharp fish bones and crustacean exoskeletons. Parents feed feathers to chicks from day one. Found in all grebes and no non-grebe species. One of the most unusual dietary behaviors in the avian world. The thick, crushing bill is adapted for hard-shelled prey -- crayfish, snails, diving beetles -- requiring the internal packing material.

nodes.title: Lobed Feet -- Not Webbed, Not Connected
nodes.description: Each toe has individual lateral flaps that expand during the power stroke and fold during recovery, reducing drag. Completely different from duck webbed feet (toes connected by continuous membrane). Arguably more efficient for diving: less drag on recovery stroke. Ducks are primarily surface swimmers; grebes are primarily divers. Foot design reflects priority: surface paddling vs. underwater propulsion. The engine now documents both solutions side by side: webbed (58-59) and lobed (60).

nodes.title: Floating Nest -- The Aquatic Nursery
nodes.description: A platform of decaying vegetation anchored to emergent reeds, rising and falling with water level. Adults cover eggs with nesting material when leaving -- staining eggs from white to brown for camouflage. Three consecutive nesting strategies: tree cavity (Wood Duck 58), ground (Mallard 59), floating platform (Grebe 60). From arboreal to terrestrial to aquatic. The Grebe cannot walk on land; it nests on water. Also carries chicks on its back while swimming -- no duck does this.

nodes.title: E=4 -- Common but Cryptic
nodes.description: Widespread on freshwater lakes and ponds but secretive and easy to miss. Prefers vegetated margins. Sinks rather than flies when approached. Brown plumage provides camouflage among reeds. Most active at dawn and dusk. Heard more often than seen. The cow-cow call carries across water; the bird remains hidden. E=4 captures the acoustic presence (real, loud) and visual absence (skulking, sinking). After E=3 (Wood Duck) and E=5 (Mallard), E=4 occupies the middle ground: louder call than the duck you cannot see, quieter call than the duck everyone sees.

nodes.title: Five Consecutive Non-Passerine Species (56-60)
nodes.description: Band-tailed Pigeon (56, Columbiformes), Belted Kingfisher (57, Coraciiformes), Wood Duck (58, Anseriformes), Mallard (59, Anseriformes), Pied-billed Grebe (60, Podicipediformes). The longest non-passerine streak in the engine. Four new orders in five degrees. Passeriformes drops from 89% (at degree 55) to 80.3% (at degree 60). The engine's taxonomic diversification is accelerating.

nodes.title: Etymology: Rump-Footed Rump-Foot
nodes.description: Podilymbus podiceps -- one of the most tautological scientific names in ornithology. Podilymbus = Podiceps + colymbus (diver). Podiceps = podex (rump) + pes (foot). The name says the same thing twice: the feet are at the rump. Compare: Aix sponsa ('bridal waterfowl,' aesthetic), Anas platyrhynchos ('flat-billed duck,' functional), Podilymbus podiceps ('rump-footed rump-foot,' anatomical). Three consecutive aquatic species, three naming philosophies descending from poetry to prose to body plan.

nodes.title: The Ben Gibbard Solo Parallel
nodes.description: Degree 60 pairs the Pied-billed Grebe with Ben Gibbard solo -- Death Cab for Cutie's frontman appearing without the band. Both are the quiet version of something bigger. Death Cab (58) is the full band; Gibbard solo (60) is the voice alone. The Mallard (59) is the loud pond generalist; the Grebe (60) is the secretive lake-edge diver. The Grebe sinks rather than flies; Gibbard goes solo rather than arena. Both choose the less visible, less energetic response. Energy drops: Death Cab E=4 to Gibbard E=2, Mallard E=5 to Grebe E=4.

nodes.title: Sister to Flamingos -- Mirandornithes
nodes.description: Molecular phylogenetics places grebes as sister to flamingos in the clade Mirandornithes. The small, drab, freshwater diver is the closest living relative of the tall, pink, saline-lake wader. Not ducks, not loons, not cormorants -- flamingos. Convergent evolution gave grebes a duck-like appearance; their actual evolutionary heritage connects them to one of the most visually dramatic birds on Earth. The engine's taxonomic surprise: the most unexpected kinship in 61 degrees.

nodes.title: Sixteenth Consecutive CSV Substitution
nodes.description: Green Frog (Lithobates clamitans, 'banjo plunk' call) replaced by Pied-billed Grebe. The sixteenth consecutive amphibian-class substitution (degrees 45-60). Lake edge habitat and E=4 energy preserved. Both are vocally distinctive from their surroundings -- the Green Frog's metallic plunk and the Grebe's cuckoo-like cow-cow are both sounds that make listeners stop and ask 'what was that?' The longest unbroken substitution streak in the engine continues.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE AQUATIC SPECIES -- ANATIDAE TO PODICIPEDIDAE. The quack (degree 59, Anas platyrhynchos, E=5, ubiquitous pond generalist, Anseriformes, webbed feet, ground nester) yields to the submerge call (degree 60, Podilymbus podiceps, E=4, secretive lake-edge diver, Podicipediformes, lobed feet, floating nest). Same ponds, entirely different evolutionary lineage. Convergent evolution documented in consecutive degrees. The audience sees what looks like a duck and learns it is not a duck.

THREE CONSECUTIVE AQUATIC SPECIES (58-59-60). Surface specialist (degree 58, Anatidae, E=3, wooded ponds, rising whistle) to surface generalist (degree 59, Anatidae, E=5, all ponds, descrescendo quack) to sustained diver (degree 60, Podicipedidae, E=4, lake edge, accelerating cow-cow). Three degrees, two orders, three aquatic strata: surface, subsurface tip, full submersion. The engine descends through the water column.

PLUNGE-DIVER vs. SUSTAINED DIVER. Kingfisher enters water to strike -- 1-2 seconds submerged, gravity-powered plunge (degree 57). Grebe lives in water -- 20-30 seconds submerged, foot-propelled pursuit hunting (degree 60). Both are fish-eating freshwater divers with completely different submersion strategies.

UNDERWATER WALKER vs. UNDERWATER SWIMMER. Dipper walks along stream bottoms gripping rocks (degree 52). Grebe swims through open water propelled by lobed feet (degree 60). Both hunt underwater through completely different methods. Three underwater strategies in the engine: walking (52), striking (57), swimming (60).

TWO SECRETIVE SPECIALISTS. Both heard more often than seen. Owl is cryptic in old-growth canopy (degree 4); Grebe is cryptic in lake-edge vegetation (degree 60). Both produce loud, distinctive, easily identified calls. Both are nearly invisible to casual observers. Both sink into their environment rather than rising above it.

Pied-billed Grebe breeds and winters on freshwater lakes and ponds with emergent vegetation throughout western Washington lowlands. Year-round resident. First Podicipedidae in the engine. New order Podicipediformes. Most common grebe in PNW. Declining in some areas due to wetland loss and degradation of emergent vegetation.

Lake-edge specialist dependent on emergent vegetation for nesting and cover. Indicator species for freshwater marsh health. Prey species for Bald Eagle, Great Blue Heron, mink. Seed disperser for aquatic plants. Cattail/bulrush removal for 'lake beautification' directly eliminates grebe habitat. Feather-eating behavior unique among engine species.
